explicit_text: My hometown is in Yamaguchi Prefecture, specifically in Iwakuni. I was born there during the Meiji period, in Meiji 17. It was a rural area where my family were farmers, and the community was close-knit with about 200 houses in our village. Growing up there shaped my early life and values, especially the importance of taking care of others.

explicit_text: I was born and raised in Iwakuni, Yamaguchi, a small farming village with about 200 houses. My family, the Sekikawas, were well-liked in the village because we always helped others when they asked for favors. Life there was simple, and I only attended grade school for four years before marrying young and eventually moving to America.

explicit_text: I was born in Meiji 17, which places my birth in the Meiji period of Japan. This was a time when Japan was modernizing, but in my village, life was still very traditional and rural. My family were farmers, and education was limited for me and my siblings. The Meiji era shaped the backdrop of my childhood and early experiences.

explicit_text: When I was a child in Japan, one of the stories I liked to tell was about Mount Fuji. The tales I shared were simple and taught lessons about being dutiful to parents, like how high Mount Fuji is or stories about animals like honey bees and crows. These stories were part of my limited education and childhood entertainment in Yamaguchi.

explicit_text: My family's religion was Buddhism, specifically the Shinshu sect. My father was a very religious man who valued taking care of people who came to our home with requests. Buddhism was an important part of our lives in Yamaguchi and influenced how we treated others in our community.

explicit_text: Our family practiced Shin Buddhism, which was the form of Buddhism we followed in Yamaguchi. explicit_text: When I traveled from Japan to America, our ship stopped in Seattle, Washington. I was very seasick during the voyage but remember the captain making sure seasick passengers were brought on deck to recover. Seattle was the first place I saw in America before continuing on to Sacramento, California.

explicit_text: After arriving in America, I went straight to Sacramento, California, where my husband had a fruit orchard. We lived on the ranch with many workers, and I did not have to cook because we hired a cook. Life in Sacramento was busy with farming, and although I had a child, I worked hard to support our family.
